## Features of Competitive and Market Intelligence Tools (Transitioning to Competitive and Market Intelligence Platforms)

Updated June 2025

### Optional Features:

* Analyze — Translate data into insights: Analyze the data gathered on markets, competitors, partners, buyers and customers to reveal insights about threats, advantages and industry trends. Conduct strength, weakness, opportunity and threat (SWOT) and other forms of analysis to derive insights and predictions from collected data.
* Exploit — Distribute insights to key stakeholders: Develop real-time dashboards, reports and summaries, automated alerts, battle cards, playbooks and training to ensure knowledge and insights are available and consumable across the enterprise. Illustrate the impact of these insights on strategic and tactical priorities and decisions.
* Gather — Collect and organize data: Collect data from multiple sources, and organize it for various stakeholders to challenge assumptions about current and future threats. Examples of internal and external data sources include CRM, win/loss analysis, employee insights, customer surveys, competitor websites, partner interviews, social media, peer review sites, job boards, syndicated research, peer-reviewed journals, industry databases, corporate reports and investor presentations.

--- SOURCE 9: Crayon Software Pricing & Plans 2026: See Your Cost ---
URL: https://www.vendr.com/marketplace/crayon

SUMMARY:
<summary>
This Vendr guide provides a comprehensive analysis of Crayon pricing in 2026, a competitive intelligence platform. Crayon uses a quote-based pricing model (no public list pricing) based on four key drivers: number of competitors tracked, user seats, feature tier (Essentials, Professional, or Enterprise), and contract term length. Based on 92 deals in Vendr's database, the median annual contract value is $29,500 (range: $12,600–$46,500+), with an average savings of 20.19%. Pricing ranges from approximately $15,000/year for small teams on Essentials (5–10 competitors, 3–10 seats) to $100,000+ for enterprise deployments (25+ competitors, 50+ seats).

The three tiers are: Essentials (entry-level, limited competitor tracking, basic analytics), Professional (10–25 competitors, advanced analytics, battlecard automation, CRM integrations), and Enterprise (25+ competitors, custom integrations, API access, dedicated CSM, professional services).

Hidden costs that can add 15–30% to base pricing include: professional services/onboarding (5–15% of ACV), battlecard customization, integrations/API access, additional competitor profiles, user seat overages, premium support tiers, and annual price escalation clauses (typically 3–7%).

Negotiation strategies include: anchoring early to budget constraints, clarifying competitor tracking scope, leveraging competitive alternatives (Klue, Kompyte/Semrush, Contify, SimilarWeb), negotiating multi-year terms with flat pricing caps, leveraging Crayon's December fiscal year-end timing, using prepayment as a lever, and capping annual increases.

Competitive comparison data shows Crayon's entry-level pricing (~15K–25K) is competitive with Klue (~20K–30K), lower than Semrush-bundled Kompyte, and slightly higher than Contify (~12K–20K). Crayon differentiates on competitor tracking depth and market intelligence, while Klue emphasizes sales enablement/battlecards, Kompyte offers Semrush bundle value, and Contify focuses on cost-effective broad market monitoring. Last updated: February 2026.
</summary>

--- SOURCE 10: Competitive Intelligence Tools: 15 Compared (2026) ---
URL: https://www.autobound.ai/blog/top-15-competitive-intelligence-tools-2026

SUMMARY:
<summary>
This Autobound article reviews the 15 best competitive intelligence (CI) tools for B2B sales teams in 2026, organized into five categories: dedicated CI platforms, market intelligence platforms, sales intelligence platforms with CI features, digital/SEO analysis tools, and win/loss/conversation intelligence tools.

**Market Context:** Per Crayon's 2025 State of CI report, 68% of B2B deals involve a direct competitor, yet teams rate themselves just 3.8/10 on competitive preparedness—costing $2-10M/year in lost deals. AI adoption in CI surged 76% YoY. Mordor Intelligence projects the CI market will reach $1.46B by 2030 (20% annual growth), and Gartner predicts 40% of tech providers will use commercial CI tools by 2026.

**Top Dedicated CI Platforms:**
- **Crayon** ($20K-$40K/yr): Most established; won 3 consecutive PMA Pulse awards (2021-2023); acquired by SoftwareOne for $1.4B in July 2025; excels at real-time tracking and Win Story Insights.
- **Klue** ($20K-$40K/yr): Best battlecard quality (9.5/10 on G2); acquired Ignition in Sept 2025; serves 250,000+ users; Compete Agent delivers agentic AI deal intelligence.
- **Kompyte by Semrush** (from $300/yr): Most budget-friendly; unlimited battlecards; 24-hour setup; reduces research from days to ~1 hour/week.

**Market Intelligence:** AlphaSense ($24K+/yr per user, $500M+ ARR, 85% of S&P 100) launched Financial Data in Nov 2025; valued at $4B (Series E, June 2024). Contify covers 700,000+ companies in 117+ languages via its Athena AI engine.

**Sales Intel with CI:** ZoomInfo (from ~$15K/yr, 1B buying signals/month) includes Chorus for conversation intelligence. 6sense (~$55K/yr median, captures 1 trillion signals daily) is a 5x Gartner ABM Leader. LinkedIn Sales Navigator ($79.99-$1,600/user/yr) is recommended as table stakes.

**Digital/SEO:** Semrush ($139.95-$499.95/mo, launched Semrush One in Oct 2025) and Similarweb ($199/mo and up) provide web traffic and ad analysis.

**Social Listening:** Brand24 ($149-$399/mo) tracks 25M sources across 90+ languages; Mention offers free entry-level monitoring.

**Win/Loss & Conversation Intelligence:** Clozd (used by 43% of CI professionals with win/loss platforms) integrates with Crayon. Gong ($1,600/user/yr + $5K platform fee, analyzes 300+ signals per call) reported an 82% win-rate lift when tracking competitor mentions. Chorus is integrated within ZoomInfo.

**ROI Data:** 71% of businesses using battlecards see higher win rates; monthly updates drive up to 59% win-rate lift (Crayon). Daily CI use yields 84% increase in competitive sales effectiveness. One case study showed win rates jumping from 32% to 67% when reps received battlecard intel within 27 minutes of competitor mentions. B2B SaaS reps save 8-12 hours/month; enterprises see 110% ROI over 3 years (NPV exceeding $1.2M).

**Recommended Stacks by Budget:**
- Starter (~$3,050/yr): Kompyte + LinkedIn Sales Navigator + Brand24 + Google Alerts
- Growth ($15K-$40K/yr): Klue or Crayon + LinkedIn + Semrush + Brand24
- Enterprise ($50K+/yr): Crayon + Clozd + AlphaSense/Contify + ZoomInfo + Gong

**Common Pitfalls:** 68% of battlecards go unused; only 26% of CI teams say reps use them as much as desired. Best practice: track only 3-5 competitors initially and integrate CI into existing CRM/Slack/email workflows rather than separate tabs.

Autobound positions signal-based selling as complementary to CI: while tools like Crayon answer "what are competitors doing?", Autobound's Signal Engine monitors 700+ buying signal types to answer "which prospects are showing competitive vulnerability right now?" and auto-personalizes outreach via its Insights Engine.

--- SOURCE 13: Crayon's 2025 State of CI report ---
URL: https://www.crayon.co/hubfs/Crayon%27s%202025%20State%20of%20CI%20Report.pdf

SUMMARY:
<summary>
The State of Competitive Intelligence 2025 (8th Edition) is Crayon's annual benchmark report based on 400+ survey responses from B2B SaaS competitive intelligence (CI) professionals collected between December 2024 and February 2025. The report reveals that markets are more competitive than ever, with 55% of companies reporting more competitive deals than the year prior and 68% of deals being head-to-head battles. Despite this, companies rate their sales rep readiness just 3.8 out of 10, resulting in an estimated $2–$10 million in lost revenue annually from competitive deals that could have been won.

Key findings include:

**AI Adoption**: AI usage in CI jumped 76% year-over-year, with AI-powered teams seeing an 82% boost in sales effectiveness. 56% of CI professionals now use AI in their work, with 60% using it daily. The top AI use cases are summarizing content (76%), analyzing competitor data (64%), and improving writing (61%).

**Sales Enablement**: 85% of CI teams enable sales, but only 48% have an executive sponsor. Teams with daily CI updates saw an 84% increase in sales effectiveness, and those with sales exec sponsors saw 76% higher effectiveness. Sales is the #1 stakeholder, rated very important by 72% of respondents.

**CI Platforms**: 54% of companies now have a dedicated CI platform (up 42% from last year), and these teams report 79% higher sales effectiveness.

# Crayon Alternative: Competitive Intelligence Without the Price Tag

![Ibby Syed](/_next/image?url=%2Fteam%2Fibby-syed.webp&w=48&q=75)Ibby Syed, Founder, Cotera

# Crayon Alternative: Do You Actually Need a CI Platform?

Crayon is one of those tools that demos beautifully. The product marketing team watches a 30-minute demo, sees automated competitor monitoring, AI-generated insights, and battlecard management all in one platform, and immediately wants it. Then someone asks about pricing. $25,000/year on the low end. Enterprise deals can run $50,000-$100,000.

For a company with a dedicated competitive intelligence team — like, actual full-time CI analysts — Crayon can justify that price. The automation saves analyst hours, the battlecard system keeps sales enablement current, and the centralized platform prevents the "competitive data is scattered across 12 different tools" problem.

For everyone else? That's a lot of money for something you can approximate with cheaper tools and a bit of process discipline.

## What Crayon Actually Does

I want to be fair about what you get at that price point. Crayon monitors competitor websites for changes (pricing, messaging, product pages). It aggregates news, social mentions, and review data into a single feed. It provides AI analysis that summarizes what competitors are doing and why it matters. And it includes a battlecard system for distributing competitive intelligence to sales teams.

The monitoring piece is where Crayon shines. It catches changes that manual monitoring misses — a small pricing page tweak, a new feature added to a comparison page, a job posting description that reveals a strategic pivot. The AI summaries turn raw monitoring data into something a human can actually process.

The battlecard system is nice but honestly replaceable. Google Docs, Notion, or your wiki can hold battlecards. Keeping them updated is a process problem, not a technology problem.

## The $0-$100/Month Alternative Stack

Here's how to cover Crayon's functionality without Crayon's price tag.

For monitoring, Google Alerts handles news mentions (free). Visualping or ChangeTower monitors competitor website changes ($10-30/month for basic plans, free tier available). G2 and Capterra send review alerts for free. LinkedIn tracks hiring signals for free.

A [market intelligence agent](/solutions/marketing/market-intelligence-agent) does the synthesis work that makes Crayon's AI summaries useful. Instead of reading 50 alerts and figuring out what matters, the agent processes the data and tells you "Competitor B changed their pricing page, moved a feature to a higher tier, and published three blog posts about enterprise security this week. They appear to be moving upmarket." That synthesis is the $25K feature. Now it's available on demand.

For social and brand monitoring, a [brand monitoring](/solutions/marketing/brand-monitoring) agent catches competitor mentions across platforms that Google Alerts misses — industry forums, niche publications, Reddit threads. Combined with [social listening alerts](/solutions/marketing/social-listening-alerts), you get coverage comparable to Crayon's social monitoring.

For news specifically, the [Newscatcher competitive news tracker](/solutions/marketing/newscatcher-competitive-news-tracker) provides deeper coverage than Google Alerts. It scans thousands of news sources and returns competitor mentions with context, sentiment, and relevance scoring.

For battlecards, use whatever your team already opens daily. If they live in Slack, put competitive snippets in a Slack channel. If they live in Notion, create a competitive database in Notion. If they live in Google Docs, make a shared drive. The tool matters less than the habit of keeping them updated.

## When Crayon-Class Platforms Earn Their Price

If your sales team has 50+ reps and competitive intelligence influences deal outcomes weekly, a platform that automates distribution matters. Sending a Slack message about a competitor change is fine for a 10-person team. It doesn't scale to 200 reps across three time zones. Crayon's push notifications and in-workflow integrations solve the distribution problem that smaller teams don't have.

If you're in a market with 10+ active competitors that all move fast (think martech, cybersecurity, HR tech), the monitoring volume overwhelms manual approaches. Google Alerts for 15 competitors generates a lot of noise. Crayon's filtering and prioritization make that volume manageable.

If competitive intelligence reporting goes to the board or investors, the polished dashboards and trend visualizations Crayon provides make a better impression than screenshots of a Google Sheet. This is a presentation problem, not an intelligence problem, but presentation matters when budget depends on it.

## The Middle Ground

Klue ($10K-$25K/year) offers similar functionality to Crayon at a lower price point, with strong battlecard management. Kompyte is another competitor in this space with slightly different strengths.

But the real middle ground is the agent-based approach. Pay nothing for standing subscriptions. Run monitoring agents weekly or monthly. Run synthesis agents before strategy reviews. Run battlecard-update agents after competitor moves. You get the intelligence without the platform cost, and you can scale up or down as needed.

The question isn't "Crayon or nothing." It's "how much competitive intelligence do I actually need, and what's the cheapest way to get it reliably?" For most B2B companies under 200 employees, the answer involves free tools, an agent for synthesis, and a Google Doc for battlecards. Not glamorous. Effective.

--- SOURCE 16: Evaluating the Top Competitive Intelligence Tools ---
URL: https://www.kompyte.com/blog/top-competitive-intelligence-tools

SUMMARY:
<summary>
This Kompyte blog post, authored by Alisa Meredith on April 3, 2024, compares three leading competitive intelligence (CI) tools: Kompyte, Crayon, and Klue. CI tools help businesses gather, filter, and distribute competitor insights across sales, marketing, and product teams. Historically done via manual screenshot comparisons, CI now relies on AI to monitor competitors' digital footprints.

**Kompyte:** Visits every competitor website and digital profile (social media, review sites, job postings, PR sites) once daily, using supervised AI trained over 8 years to classify changes and filter noise. Captures 500+ million data points including competitor websites, Google News, PRNewswire, Glassdoor/Indeed/LinkedIn job postings, G2/Capterra/TrustRadius reviews, app stores, SERP/SEO monitoring, AdWords, and Crunchbase. Offers unlimited Battlecards integrated with HubSpot and Salesforce, two-way Salesforce integration for win/loss analysis, InMail win-loss interviews, GPT Auto Summarize for instant takeaways, and appears to be the most affordable. Strengths: mature AI, speed (enables teams in ~1 hour/week), and recent Semrush acquisition. Best for those wanting minimal filtering time.

**Crayon:** Self-described as helping revenue teams compete. Relies largely on visual (screenshot) comparisons rather than daily deep crawls, though has added an early-stage AI component requiring human confirmation. Tracks news/PR, social media, review sites, forums, app stores, hiring platforms, and filing sites (no ad/SEO tracking). Offers unlimited Battlecards (built from scratch, no templates) and integrated with Gong, Slack, Salesforce. Recently partnered with Primary Intelligence for win/loss analysis. Strengths: market longevity, brand recognition, unlimited users, strong CS team. Appears to be the most expensive; charges for support, adding/changing competitors, and integrations. Best for companies with few, stable competitors (initial setup ~3 weeks).

**Klue:** Uses an automated web crawler focused on news/press releases, with relatively young AI filtering ~87% of noise. Tracks website changes, reviews, social media, press mentions, product launches. Known for visually appealing Battlecards and many integrations. Acquired DoubleCheck for win/loss data/interview recordings. Charges for setup, separate fees for curators vs. consumers, and likely extra for integrations. Pricing falls between Kompyte and Crayon. Best for sales-enablement-only use cases with a dedicated curator.

The post concludes by recommending Kompyte for its real-time monitoring, mature AI, actionable insights, and broad integrations, though it acknowledges Kompyte's inherent bias as the article's publisher.
</summary>

# Klue Pricing (2026): What It Really Costs + Total Cost of Ownership

Last updated:

![Klue competitive intelligence pricing explained — entry cost, contract model, and total cost of ownership](/_next/image?url=%2Fimages%2Fblog%2Fblog_image11.png&w=3840&q=75&dpl=dpl_8EEnzM9LE6Escco2VXdjShGDBEqK)

Klue uses custom, sales-led pricing with no public rate card. Based on publicly available third-party procurement sources, entry deployments typically land around $15,000–$20,000+ per year, with enterprise configurations scaling significantly higher depending on seats, competitors tracked, and integration scope. Klue is usually cited as slightly more expensive than Crayon and meaningfully more expensive than Kompyte. The license, however, is rarely the largest line item: Klue assumes a dedicated owner (a PMM or competitive enablement lead) to author and maintain battlecards, and that labor is typically the biggest part of the real total cost of ownership. If you don't have that owner, a transparent continuous-monitoring platform like Parano.ai (from €89/month) is usually the better-value answer.

## What does Klue cost in 2026?

Klue does not publish pricing. There's no rate card on the site, no self-service tier, and no public price points — every deal is a custom, sales-led contract disclosed after a discovery call. That's standard for the enterprise competitive intelligence category, but it makes budgeting hard before you're already in a sales cycle.

Based on publicly available third-party procurement and review sources, here's the realistic picture as of 2026.

## Klue pricing at a glance

| Detail |
| --- |
| **Entry pricing (annual)** | ~$15,000–$20,000+ per year (per third-party sources) |
| **Pricing model** | Custom, sales-led — no public rate card |
| **Free trial** | ❌ Demo only |
| **Enterprise** | Scales significantly higher with seats, competitors, and integrations |
| **Relative position** | Slightly above Crayon; meaningfully above Kompyte |
| **Hidden cost** | Dedicated owner (PMM / competitive enablement lead) |

Figures are directional, drawn from third-party procurement and review sites rather than an official Klue rate card. Treat them as a starting point for a negotiation, not a quote.

## What drives the price

Klue's custom pricing flexes on a few axes:

* **Seats** — how many users (especially reps consuming battlecards) need access.
* **Competitors tracked** — broader competitive sets cost more to monitor and maintain.
* **Integration scope** — Salesforce, Slack, and enablement-tool integrations factor into the contract tier.
* **Support and onboarding** — enterprise configurations bundle implementation and customer success.

Because none of this is public, two companies of similar size can sign meaningfully different contracts. Negotiation matters more here than a published list price would suggest.

## The real cost: total cost of ownership

The license is rarely the biggest number. Klue is an enterprise deployment built around a **human-curated workflow** — someone authors the battlecards, keeps competitor coverage current, and runs the enablement cadence. That someone is usually a product marketer or competitive enablement lead, and their loaded cost typically exceeds the Klue license itself.

This is the single most common way Klue budgets go wrong: the team approves the license, then discovers that without a dedicated owner the battlecards go stale and the deployment drifts toward shelfware within a quarter or two. If you're pricing Klue, price the owner alongside it — that's the honest total cost of ownership.

## How Klue pricing compares

Klue sits in the enterprise CI suite tier alongside Crayon and Kompyte:

* **Klue vs Crayon** — similar entry range (~$15–20k/year), with Klue often slightly higher. Klue is sales-first; Crayon is broader CI. Full breakdown: [Klue vs Crayon (2026)](/blog/klue-vs-crayon).
* **Klue vs Kompyte** — Kompyte (Semrush) is consistently cheaper, especially if you already pay for Semrush. Full breakdown: [Klue vs Kompyte (2026)](/blog/klue-vs-kompyte).
* For the whole category priced side by side, see [the 12 best competitive intelligence tools in 2026](/blog/the-best-competitive-intelligence-products-in-2026).

## A transparent, lower-cost alternative

If the honest answer to "who will own this?" is "nobody has the time," Klue is the wrong tool regardless of price — it's built for teams running a formal enablement program with a dedicated owner.

We built [Parano.ai](/) for the other case: continuous competitive monitoring with AI summarization, delivered to Slack or email, with **public pricing from €89/month** and no curation overhead. It's not a battlecard authoring suite — if formal battlecards run by a PMM are the job, evaluate Klue on its own terms. But if you want competitor change awareness without a five-figure contract and a dedicated headcount, the math is very different.

What AlphaSense Does

## What does AlphaSense actually do?

[AlphaSense](https://www.alphasense.com/) is a [market intelligence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Market_intelligence) platform that indexes [SEC filings](https://www.sec.gov/edgar/search/), [earnings call transcripts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earnings_call), broker research, news, and expert call transcripts. It serves institutional finance, large consulting firms, and Fortune 500 corporate strategy teams. AlphaSense raised a Series F in 2024 at a $4 billion valuation and is rated highly on G2.

The expert call transcript inventory comes from the 2024 Stream Research Group acquisition that followed AlphaSense's 2022 purchase of Sentieo. Reviews on G2 are concentrated among hedge funds, investment banks, and corporate strategy functions where the platform's search-across-corpus model fits a daily-use research workflow.

Total cost scales by seat count. A typical 3-seat deployment runs $45,000 to $60,000 per year on the standard tier. Adding expert call access for those 3 seats pushes the annual to roughly $120,000+. Multi-team deployments at hedge funds and investment banks routinely exceed $250,000 per year.

Where AlphaSense Falls Short

## Where does AlphaSense actually fall short?

### Research surface, not synthesis

AlphaSense compresses the document-retrieval and search-across-corpus phase of research, but it does not produce strategic recommendations. The analyst seat reading the platform still has to interpret what's there, build the financial model, and write the brief leadership will act on. AlphaSense is a productivity multiplier for an existing research function, not a substitute for one.

### Source coverage tilted toward institutional finance

The corpus is excellent for SEC filings, earnings calls, broker research, and expert call transcripts — the document set that hedge funds and investment banks read. Reddit, app store reviews, Trustpilot patterns, niche forums, and other consumer-discussion surfaces are not the focus. For categories where the unprompted-customer voice is the differentiator, AlphaSense alone is incomplete.

### Per-seat economics for occasional research needs

At $15,000 to $50,000 per seat per year, AlphaSense is structured for organizations with daily-use research workflows. Operating companies that have 4 to 8 strategic intelligence questions per year would pay roughly $2,000-$6,000 per question if a single seat is amortized across that volume — far higher than per-report pricing from done-for-you research firms.

### Annual contracts plus seat lock-in

Standard enterprise contracts are annual with multi-seat minimums. Cancellation, seat reduction, or scope changes typically require waiting until renewal. For organizations whose research needs vary by quarter, the structural inflexibility is a recurring friction point in G2 reviews.

Common Questions

## Common questions about AlphaSense and how we compare?

How much does AlphaSense cost?

AlphaSense does not publish exact public pricing. Industry sources and broker comparisons indicate seat-based subscriptions running roughly $15,000 to $20,000 per seat per year for the standard Enterprise tier, $40,000+ per seat per year for tiers that include expert call transcripts (the former Stream Research Group inventory acquired in 2024), and custom contracts for enterprise deployments at multi-hundred-thousand-dollar annual totals. Confirmation directly with AlphaSense is required for current rates.

Is AlphaSense worth the seat price?

For institutional investors, hedge funds, investment banks, large consulting firms, and corporate strategy teams that read SEC filings and earnings calls daily, yes. The platform consolidates years of filings, calls, and broker research with strong AI-powered search across the corpus. For organizations that have 4 to 8 strategic intelligence questions per year and don't need always-on access to financial-research infrastructure, done-for-you intelligence reports deliver more value at a small fraction of the seat cost.

Does AlphaSense replace a research analyst?

No. AlphaSense compresses the time analysts spend retrieving documents and surfacing relevant passages, but synthesis, financial modeling, and strategic recommendation work still require a human. Most AlphaSense customers run a research function with multiple seats, where the platform is a productivity multiplier rather than a headcount substitute.

How does AlphaSense compare to Crayon, Klue, or Contify?

Different category. AlphaSense indexes financial filings, earnings call transcripts, expert call transcripts, and broker research for institutional finance and corporate strategy use cases. Crayon and Klue track competitor websites, social, and ad campaigns for product marketing and sales enablement. Contify aggregates news and regulatory filings across tracked entities for market intelligence teams. AlphaSense's source coverage and enterprise pricing put it in the institutional-finance bucket, not the SaaS competitive-intelligence bucket.

Does AlphaSense cover consumer sentiment and forum data?

AlphaSense's primary corpus is structured business and financial sources: filings, earnings calls, broker research, expert calls, news, and regulatory documents. Reddit, app store reviews, Trustpilot patterns, niche forums, and other consumer-discussion surfaces are not the platform's focus. For a complete picture that combines financial signal with consumer voice, you'll need supplementary tools or services covering the unprompted-customer surface.

## The category is drifting upmarket — away from the mid-market

The incumbents are chasing enterprise budgets, and the signals are everywhere we look. [AlphaSense crossed $500M ARR](/reports/alphasense-surpasses-500m-arr-may-2026-af829687) as a finance-and-enterprise research platform — useful, but not built for GTM competitive intelligence. [Contify moved its HQ to New York and Comintelli repositioned to “intelligence as a solution”](/reports/contify-moves-hq-to-new-york-and-comintelli-shifts-to-intelligence-as--558fb721), both reaching for larger contracts. Valona sells to the Global 2000.

The pattern: every dedicated CI tool got more expensive and more enterprise this year. The mid-market PMM — the buyer who needs competitive intelligence most and has the least budget for an analyst-run platform — is the customer this category is quietly leaving behind.

## Competitive intelligence just got its first Magic Quadrant

The category is consolidating and credentialing at the same time. In its inaugural CI Magic Quadrant, Gartner named [both Klue and Crayon Leaders](/compare/klue-vs-crayon) (May 2026). When a category gets its first Magic Quadrant, two things tend to follow: prices rise and the long tail gets acquired — Kompyte is already owned by Semrush, and Valona merged with A-INSIGHTS.

## Every tool now ships an AI agent — so “AI-powered” stopped sorting them

Through 2026 the category converged on one message: agentic AI that does the research for you. In our own weekly monitoring of these tools we watched it happen in step — Crayon moved to autonomous AI agents, Contify paired its agentic AI with human-in-the-loop verification, and AlphaSense rebuilt around an AI-native research workflow. The pitch is now near-identical across the field.

Which means it no longer separates them. When every platform says “AI-powered,” the question that actually sorts them is whether you can trust what the AI hands back — is each claim cited to a source and scored for confidence, or are you taking a generated summary on faith? The most common complaint we see in the sentiment around the incumbents is noise and alert fatigue, not a shortage of AI. That is the gap IndustryLens is built for: every claim in the weekly briefing links to its source and carries a confidence score, so a rep can repeat it in a live deal without getting caught out.

## What each tool is actually for

Stripped of the homepage language, here is the job each tool is really built to do:

* **[Klue](/alternatives/klue)** — competitive enablement for enterprise sales orgs that want battlecards inside the Salesforce flow; the one tool with productized win-loss (DoubleCheck).
* **[Crayon](/alternatives/crayon)** — broad automated signal capture for enterprise CI teams with the headcount to work it.
* **[Kompyte](/alternatives/kompyte)** — sales-first auto-updated battlecards, now bundled inside Semrush.

* **[Valona Intelligence](/alternatives/valona)** — analyst-augmented market intelligence for Global 2000 strategy teams.
* **[AlphaSense](/alternatives/alphasense)** — enterprise market and financial research; powerful, but adjacent to GTM CI rather than built for it.
* **IndustryLens** — automated, cited competitive intelligence for mid-market B2B SaaS, delivered as one weekly briefing across 350+ sources, priced from €59/month.

Half of these aren’t really competing with each other: Klue, Crayon and Kompyte fight over B2B SaaS sales teams; Valona, Contify and AlphaSense serve strategy and finance. If you’re weighing two of them directly, see [Klue vs Crayon](/compare/klue-vs-crayon) or [Crayon vs Kompyte](/compare/crayon-vs-kompyte).

## The three questions that actually narrow the field

Forget feature checklists. Three questions sort these tools faster than any grid:

* **Will they tell you the price?** Only one of seven will without a sales call. If transparent, self-serve pricing matters, the field narrows immediately.
* **Do you need a person to run it?** The enterprise platforms assume a dedicated CI analyst. If you don’t have one, that’s a hidden cost on top of the contract.
* **Is it built for sales, PMM, or strategy?** Klue leans sales enablement; Crayon leans PMM; Valona/Contify/AlphaSense lean strategy and research. Buy for the team that will actually use it.

## Common questions

### Which competitive intelligence tools publish their pricing?

As of mid-2026, of the seven dedicated CI tools we track, only IndustryLens publishes a price (from €59/month). Klue, Crayon, Kompyte, Contify, Valona Intelligence and AlphaSense are all quote-only or opaque — pricing requires a sales call, and Contify and Comintelli put a lead-capture form where the price would be.

### How much do Klue and Crayon cost?

Neither publishes pricing — both are quote-only and require a demo. Independent estimates put dedicated enterprise CI platforms in the ~€20K–€40K/year range, but the only honest answer is that you have to take a sales call to find out. IndustryLens publishes from €59/month, no demo gate.

### What is the best competitive intelligence tool for a mid-market SaaS company?

It depends on who owns CI. Klue suits enterprise sales orgs that want battlecards in the Salesforce flow; Crayon suits enterprise CI teams wanting maximum-breadth monitoring; AlphaSense is really enterprise/finance research. For a mid-market PMM or sales-enablement team (200–1,000 employees) that wants cited intelligence without an analyst or a sales call, a purpose-built mid-market tool like IndustryLens fits — published pricing, 350+ sources, one weekly cited briefing.

### Is AlphaSense a competitive intelligence tool?

AlphaSense is an enterprise market and financial-intelligence platform — strong across filings, expert transcripts and research, now past $500M ARR. It is adjacent to dedicated CI rather than a direct B2B SaaS competitive-intelligence tool, and its pricing and buyer profile sit firmly in enterprise/finance, not mid-market GTM.

### Which CI tools include win-loss analysis?

Among the dedicated tools, Klue is the one that has productized win-loss — and it has built that surface largely through acquisition: DoubleCheck (2022), Goldpan.ai (AI-driven win-loss research, 2025) and Ignition (an agentic product-marketing platform, acquired for its IP in 2026), all folded into Klue’s Compete and Win-Loss products. It is a consolidation play — more surface area, still enterprise-priced and quote-only. Most others, including IndustryLens, focus on continuous competitive monitoring rather than running win-loss interview programs. If win-loss is your core need, weigh it as a distinct requirement.

### What is the best Klue or Crayon alternative?

It depends what you are escaping — usually price, the demo gate, or needing a dedicated analyst. For mid-market teams that want published pricing and a cited weekly briefing without enterprise overhead, IndustryLens is a direct alternative. See our Klue alternatives and Crayon alternatives guides for the full vendor-by-vendor picture.

### What are the main competitive intelligence companies?

The dedicated competitive-intelligence vendors we track every week are Klue and Crayon (the two Gartner named Leaders in its inaugural CI Magic Quadrant, May 2026), Kompyte (owned by Semrush), Contify and Comintelli, Valona Intelligence (merged with A-INSIGHTS), and IndustryLens. AlphaSense sits adjacent as an enterprise market- and financial-intelligence platform. Of these, only IndustryLens publishes a price — the rest are quote-only — so the practical shortlist depends as much on sales motion and budget as on features.

### Competitive intelligence you can actually read every Monday.

350+ sources, one weekly cited briefing, published from €59/month. No demo gate.

--- SOURCE 15: How to use AI as a competitive intelligence tool | Dcipher Analytics ---
URL: https://www.dcipheranalytics.com/blog/how-to-use-ai-as-a-competitive-intelligence-tool

SUMMARY:
<summary>
This article from Dcipher Analytics explores how artificial intelligence is transforming competitive intelligence (CI). The digital information universe exceeded 1 zettabyte in 2009 and is projected to exceed 40 zettabytes by next year (2027), with most data being unstructured (text, images, video, audio). This presents both an opportunity and a challenge for CI professionals who need tools to extract strategic insights from this dispersed information.

Competitive intelligence is defined as gathering insights about the external business environment—products, customers, competitors, substitutes, and supply chains—to support strategic decisions. The typical CI cycle involves planning research questions, collecting information, processing/analyzing it, and disseminating insights.

AI impacts CI in three main ways:

1. **Expanding domains for insight gathering**: Deep learning-powered NLP can now recognize sentiment, emotions, entities, categories, and topics with human precision across news, social media, analyst reports, patents, and academic articles. AI has also closed the gap in speech recognition (enabling analysis of customer support calls and podcasts) and object recognition (enabling visual trend spotting, brand scanning, and image-based market insights).

2. **Changing how information is searched and processed**: AI shifts information search from manual, criteria-based approaches to cognitive, adaptive search that understands contextual similarity. In analysis, it replaces statistical/hypothesis-based methods with open-ended deep learning approaches that cluster texts and images into a navigable 'landscape of meaning,' allowing CI professionals to see both the forest and the trees.

3. **Enabling new ways of disseminating insights**: Natural language generation automates insight codification. Knowledge graphs continuously extract and structure information from various sources, enabling integration with conversational interfaces like chatbots.

The article notes a shift in time allocation: traditionally CI professionals spent 80% of time collecting/processing information and 20% making sense of it. With AI, these proportions are flipping—professionals now teach models to capture relevant information and use human knowledge to interpret deep learning findings rather than managing static search criteria.

A case study features Covestro (formerly Bayer MaterialScience), which used Dcipher Analytics to map China's startup ecosystem by structuring investment deal data into an ownership network, helping identify promising startups for collaboration.
</summary>

<key_excerpts>
The vast majority of this data is unstructured, particularly in the form of text, images, video, and audio. This constitutes a huge opportunity and an equally huge challenge to the competitive intelligence professional. Having tools and processes in place for continuously and effortlessly extracting strategic decision support from the digital information universe is a real competitive advantage. New, deep learning-powered techniques are now able to recognize sentiment, emotions, entities, categories, and topics in text with human precision. If they traditionally spent 80% of the time on collecting and processing information and the remaining 20% making sense of it and disseminating the insights, these proportions are being flipped with the new, AI-powered paradigm.
</key_excerpts>

--- SOURCE 17: Contify AI Market Intelligence Platform: Features & Review | Debriefing ---
URL: https://debriefing.io/competitive-profiles/contify

SUMMARY:
<summary>
Contify is an AI-driven market and competitive intelligence (CI) platform founded in 2013, positioned between free manual monitoring (e.g., Google Alerts) and enterprise CI platforms like Klue, Crayon, and Kompyte. Mid-market pricing starts at ~$500/month, with mid-tier plans typically running $1,000–$3,000/month for teams of 5–10 — a fraction of the $25,000–$70,000 enterprise contracts.

**Core capabilities:**
- Intelligence monitoring across 1M+ sources, including news, press releases, competitor websites (pricing pages, product pages, careers, blogs), social media (LinkedIn, Twitter/X), and regulatory/financial filings. Coverage is strongest in the US, Western Europe, and the tech sector.
- Custom taxonomy system — Contify's most distinctive feature — allowing teams to define custom tags, categories, and routing rules to direct different intelligence types (e.g., pricing to sales, regulatory to legal) to the right stakeholders.
- Automated stakeholder newsletters that push scheduled intelligence briefings via email to executives, sales managers, and board members who don't log into the platform.
- Basic battlecard and competitor profile capabilities, though considered functional but shallow compared to Klue's purpose-built battlecard editor.
- Salesforce integration oriented around alerting rather than deep battlecard delivery.

**Best fit:** Mid-market companies with limited CI budgets; strategy, marketing, and business development teams; organizations needing complex intelligence routing across multiple business units; and teams prioritizing automated stakeholder briefings.

**Not ideal for:** Sales-first teams needing high-quality battlecards (Klue is stronger); programs tracking 20+ competitors needing advanced AI signal prioritization (Crayon is stronger); and organizations needing built-in win/loss analysis (Contify lacks this). Contify also lacks win/loss analysis capabilities and has weaker coverage in Southeast Asia, South Asia, and Latin America.
</summary>

<key_excerpts>
Contify's positioning sits between two distinct markets: above the 'just use Google Alerts' tier, and below the $25,000+ enterprise battlecard platforms. This middle market — teams that have outgrown manual monitoring but are not ready for a full enterprise CI commitment — is Contify's primary growth opportunity.

The custom taxonomy system is genuinely powerful for organizations that need different teams to receive different intelligence cuts from the same monitoring setup. If your CI program needs to serve six business units with different competitive landscapes, Contify's taxonomy handles this more flexibly than rigid platform structures.

Contify is not primarily a sales enablement tool — it is an intelligence distribution tool. Teams that need to monitor competitors and distribute findings to stakeholders across multiple functions will benefit more from Contify's taxonomy and newsletter capabilities than teams primarily building sales battlecards.

For teams spending more than four hours per week on manual competitive monitoring, Contify's lower pricing tiers typically pay for themselves in analyst time saved.

Against Crayon, Contify offers more pricing flexibility and better taxonomy-based organization, but Crayon's AI signal scoring, automated categorization, and intelligence breadth are more sophisticated.
</key_excerpts>

## Frequently asked questions

### What kind of intelligence does Contify deliver to IT/ ITes/ SaaS companies?

Contify monitors over 1Mn+ sources, including company’s owned digital channels (website, youtube, social media), technology publications (e.g., TechCrunch, Venturebeat), product review platforms (G2, Capterra), patents, developer forums, and earnings transcripts, to deliver actionable insights on product launches, pricing changes, partnerships, cybersecurity incidents, GenAI developments, cloud infrastructure updates, and more.

### How is Contify different from Google Alerts?

Contify is purpose-built for market and competitive intelligence. Unlike Google Alerts, which only capture basic mentions from select web pages and miss most strategic updates, Contify uses a deep business-grade content sourcing mechanism. It employs AI for contextual relevance, custom taxonomies, and multilingual coverage, enabling role-specific real-time delivery of intelligence. This ensures you never miss critical signals that generic keyword alerts overlook.

### How is Contify different from generic AI tools like ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Gemini?

Contify offers automated, end-to-end workflows that proactively synthesize, organize, and distribute decision-ready insights from curated business-grade sources. Unlike generic AI tools, which provide high-level, ad-hoc outputs and risk hallucinations, Contify integrates company context, advanced taxonomies, internal data, and multilingual monitoring. It eliminates manual prompt engineering, delivers highly accurate, traceable intelligence, making it vastly superior for ongoing, strategic market monitoring and competitive intelligence programs.

### In our organization, who can benefit from Contify’s M&CI platform?

Contify empowers all business teams, including Leadership, Strategy, Product, Marketing, and Sales. It provides them with timely, role-specific insights to refine product roadmaps, sharpen competitive positioning, and increase deal win rates. M&CI analysts use its AI capabilities (like Ask Athena) to automate data collection, answer ad-hoc queries, and accelerate strategic recommendations.

### How long does it take to implement Contify?

Contify is known for offering a quick and comprehensive setup service. Our team manages the full platform setup for you, allowing you to start leveraging its capabilities within 5 to 7 working days of onboarding.

### Can we integrate Contify with our existing internal tools and systems?

Yes. Contify provides seamless integration with many external tools and systems. These include Salesforce, Microsoft SharePoint, Slack, Microsoft Teams, and BI platforms via APIs and webhooks, ensuring intelligence flows directly into your existing workflows

--- SOURCE 1: How to use AI as a competitive intelligence tool | Dcipher Analytics ---
URL: https://www.dcipheranalytics.com/blog/how-to-use-ai-as-a-competitive-intelligence-tool

SUMMARY:
<summary>
This article explores how AI is transforming competitive intelligence (CI) by enabling professionals to extract strategic insights from the exponentially growing digital information universe. The total amount of digital information exceeded 1 zettabyte in 2009 (equivalent to 100 million times the U.S. Library of Congress) and is expected to exceed 40 zettabytes by next year, with most of it being unstructured (text, images, video, audio).

Competitive intelligence involves gathering insights about the external business environment—products, customers, competitors, substitutes, and supply chains—to support strategic decisions. The traditional CI cycle includes planning, collecting, processing/analyzing, and disseminating insights.

AI impacts CI in three main ways:

1. **Expanding insight-gathering domains**: Deep learning-powered natural language understanding now recognizes sentiment, emotions, entities, categories, and topics in text with human precision. Speech recognition has matured enough to process customer support calls, podcasts, and broadcasts. Object recognition enables analysis of images and video for trend spotting, brand scanning, and market insights.

2. **Changing information search and processing**: AI shifts from manual keyword-based search to cognitive, adaptive search that understands contextual similarity and learns from user feedback. In analysis, deep learning clusters texts and images into landscapes of meaning, enabling visual navigation between overview and detail.

3. **Enabling new dissemination methods**: Natural language generation automates insight writing, while automated dashboards and alerts are customized for different organizational roles. Knowledge graphs structure information from multiple sources, enabling integration with chatbots and conversational interfaces.

The AI-powered paradigm is flipping the traditional time allocation: CI professionals used to spend 80% on collecting/processing and 20% on sense-making/dissemination, but now these proportions are reversing. Professionals now train deep learning models rather than define static search criteria, use AI to surface patterns, and leverage automated dissemination tools.

A case study features Covestro (formerly Bayer MaterialScience), which used Dcipher Analytics to map the Chinese startup ecosystem by structuring data on hundreds of thousands of investment deals into a network of ownership relations, helping identify promising startups for collaboration.
</summary>

- Enterprise ($50K+/yr): Crayon + Clozd + AlphaSense/Contify + ZoomInfo + Gong

The article closes by positioning Autobound's Signal Engine (700+ signal types from 35+ sources) as complementary to CI tools—monitoring prospect behavior for competitive vulnerability signals (news mentions, tech-stack changes, job postings, funding events) that indicate readiness to switch vendors.

This article, written by Julie Currid (Co-Founder & Marketing Lead at BattleReady), benchmarks the top five competitive intelligence (CI) platforms for B2B SaaS in 2026: Klue, Crayon, Kompyte, Cipher, and BattleReady. The author frames the category as splitting into two waves: legacy content-management tools built for product marketing teams (PMM), and a newer wave of AI-native operating systems designed for entire go-to-market (GTM) teams.

The five platforms were evaluated across five weighted dimensions: always-on capture, AI synthesis, CRM/Slack activation, win/loss feedback loops, and total cost of ownership.

Key platform findings:
- **Klue**: Best for enterprise PMM teams with dedicated CI headcount. Strong battlecard authoring, deep Salesforce integration, and clean source curation. List pricing typically $50k+ ACV with 8–12 week implementation.

- **Kompyte** (now part of Semrush): Best for lean PMM teams (1–2 people). Price-friendly with strong capture, but lighter activation. Customers tend to outgrow it.
- **Cipher**: Best for teams needing affordable battlecards plus light monitoring. Solid value play with limited AI.
- **BattleReady**: Best for lean GTM teams wanting enterprise-grade intelligence without overhead. (Note: full disclosure, the author works for this company.) Built to bridge Klue-grade depth with modern AI-native speed. Captures continuously, synthesizes per-deal, activates natively in CRM and Slack, and closes the loop with win/loss data. Implementation takes days, not quarters.

The decision matrix compares AI synthesis, CRM depth, and cost across all platforms. The article recommends asking vendors to demo against a live deal in the pipeline rather than a sandbox.

--- SOURCE 8: Comintelli acquires US competitor Cipher’s assets for the K360 SaaS Platform - Comintelli ---
URL: https://news.cision.com/comintelli/r/comintelli-acquires-us-competitor-cipher-s-assets-for-the-k360-saas-platform,c3694959

SUMMARY:
<summary>
On January 9, 2023, Comintelli, a Swedish Market & Competitive Intelligence (M&CI) software company, announced the acquisition of assets from American competitor Cipher related to the SaaS platform Knowledge360® (K360). The acquisition includes Intellectual Property Rights to the K360 platform and access to Cipher's existing customer base. Knowledge360® will cease as a service, with customers offered conversion to Comintelli's Intelligence2day® platform, aiming to grow Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR).

Comintelli's ARR was SEK 21.3 million at the end of Q3 2022, while Cipher's K360 operation had a slightly lower ARR. The acquisition was financed through loans and cash on hand, including a SEK 3.5 million new issue conducted in summer 2022 to explore M&A possibilities, plus a SEK 2 million loan from existing shareholders at 1% monthly interest with a 5% setup fee, expiring December 31, 2023. Exact purchase terms were not disclosed; the price is based on customers converting from K360 to Intelligence2day®.

The acquisition strengthens Comintelli's position in the US market, particularly in the Pharmaceutical & Healthcare and Insurance sectors. Both platforms were recognized as significant players in the M&CI landscape by a recent Forrester report, with Comintelli previously focused on Europe and Cipher on the US market.
</summary>

<key_excerpts>
"Through this acquisition, Comintelli becomes a strong global player in M&CI, actively participating in the industry's consolidation. We see a positive future growth potential, where both we and our customers can benefit from advantages of scale," — Jesper Martell, CEO of Comintelli AB.

"Comintelli's and Cipher's platforms meet very similar customer needs... As the market for M&CI platforms continues to mature, we believe our customers are best served by a platform that can deliver against these key needs with additional scale. This new combination will provide just such a platform." — Peter Grimm, President of Cipher.

"North America is a highly prioritized market for Comintelli where we have had our own organization in the USA for several years. The acquisition of K360 assets further strengthens our position in the USA, especially in the Pharmaceutical & Healthcare and Insurance sectors," — Christian Bjersér, CEO of Comintelli Inc.

# Cipher and Securonix Partner to Deliver AI-Powered Managed Security Services

![Cipher, a Prosegur company, is a leading managed security services (MSS) provider, renowned for its world-class managed detection and response (xMDR) service.]( "Cipher, a Prosegur company, is a leading managed security services (MSS) provider, renowned for its world-class managed detection and response (xMDR) service.")

Sep 10, 2025, 09:11 ET

## Share this article

![](data:image/jpg;base64,)

Share this article

![](data:image/jpg;base64,)

*Partnership Combines World-class SOC Expertise with AI-driven SIEM to Help Channel Partners Expand Security Offerings, Accelerate Growth, and Strengthen Client Protection*

DEERFIELD BEACH, Fla., Sept. 10, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- [Cipher](https://edge.prnewswire.com/c/link/?t=0&l=en&o=4506476-1&h=3636137770&u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.cipher.com%2F&a=Cipher) — a global leader in managed security services and managed threat detection and response — and [Securonix](https://edge.prnewswire.com/c/link/?t=0&l=en&o=4506476-1&h=2645315631&u=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.securonix.com%2F&a=Securonix) — a pioneer in AI-driven Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) — have formed a strategic partnership to deliver advanced managed security services tailored for channel partners. This collaboration equips partners with scalable, AI-powered solutions to address the growing challenges of threat detection, response, and regulatory compliance.

*"We're thrilled to partner with Securonix to expand the reach of our managed security services," said Cipher North America CEO Tom Smith. "This collaboration enables partners to deliver world-class, AI-driven security operations that protect their customers from evolving threats while driving new business opportunities."*

According to Smith, the combined strength of Cipher's global managed SOC expertise and Securonix's AI-powered SIEM technology delivers a comprehensive solution.

"This collaboration enhances real-time threat detection, accelerates incident response, and streamlines compliance management — giving organizations a smarter and more efficient path to securing their environments," said Smith.

The two companies say the benefits for channel partners include:

*"Securonix is committed to advancing cybersecurity innovation, and this partnership with Cipher strengthens that mission,"* said Securonix Chief Revenue Officer Scott Sampson. *"By combining our leading SIEM platform with Cipher's SOC expertise, we're providing channel partners with a comprehensive, integrated solution for their customers that accelerates growth and delivers exceptional value to end-users."*

For more information, visit: [cipher.com](https://edge.prnewswire.com/c/link/?t=0&l=en&o=4506476-1&h=3124154858&u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.cipher.com%2F&a=cipher.com)

Cipher, a Prosegur company, is a leading managed security services (MSS) provider, renowned for its world-class managed detection and response (xMDR) service. Cipher meets you where you are with flexible and open support for your technology needs, providing localized expertise and global reach. For more information visit: [cipher.com](https://edge.prnewswire.com/c/link/?t=0&l=en&o=4506476-1&h=3124154858&u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.cipher.com%2F&a=cipher.com)

**About Securonix**

Securonix is leading the transformation of cybersecurity with the industry's first Unified Defense SIEM powered by agentic AI, purpose-built to decide and act across the threat lifecycle with a human-in-the-loop philosophy. Built for scale, precision, and speed, our cloud-native platform empowers global enterprises to shift from reactive security to proactive, autonomous operations. Recognized as a Leader in the Gartner® Magic Quadrant™ for SIEM and a Customers' Choice by Gartner Peer Insights™, Securonix is driving the next era of intelligent, autonomous security operations. --- SOURCE 11: AI in Competitive Intelligence Report 2026 - Klue ---
URL: https://klue.com/ai-in-competitive-intelligence-report-2026

SUMMARY:
<summary>
This webpage is Klue's "AI in Competitive Intelligence Report — 2026," which explores how competitive intelligence (CI) and product marketing (PMM) teams are building AI workflows and agents. The report, based on a survey of 250+ CI and PMM professionals, is structured in four chapters.

**Key Finding:** The report warns that many AI CI builds are "brainless"—lacking the two critical layers needed to produce trustworthy outputs: an Intelligence Layer and a Context Layer.

**Chapter 1 - The build movement is real:** 97% of CI teams are actively building or planning AI workflows. The top drivers are replacing slow manual processes (50%), proactive innovation (27%), headcount pressure (24%), leadership mandates (18%), and competitive urgency (10%). Top AI use cases include summarizing competitor activity (83%), generating battlecards (83%), and refining competitive positioning (78%). However, 43% report their AI system isn't increasing outputs.

**Chapter 2 - The two missing layers:**
- Intelligence Layer: 87% rely on static data sources, 81% don't know how fresh their data is, and only 12% systematically weight data sources. 47% treat all sources equally with no weighting.
- Context Layer: 82% lack a systematic process for human expertise to correct the system. Only 51% have their ICP and positioning built into the system, and 36% can actively steer source prioritization.

**Chapter 3 - When builds go brainless:** 76% have had AI CI outputs they couldn't stand behind, and 79% don't trust outputs to go directly to sellers (only 2% fully trust them with no review). Problems include: confident-sounding answers with weak sources (73%), generic outputs not tailored to deals (60%), inability to distinguish real threats (36%), outdated information (28%), and contradictory outputs (20%). 53% require manual review before sellers see outputs, which undermines the scale AI promised.

**Chapter 4 - What separates survivors:** Teams with structured intelligence layers are 2.5× more likely to trust outputs to sellers, 5.7× less likely to struggle with inconsistent quality, and 0% reported outdated data failures. Teams with structured context layers are 2.9× less likely to report major challenges, 3.6× more likely to have ICP/positioning reflected in outputs, and 20% reported 3x+ output volume. To fully trust AI CI outputs, professionals want: verifiable sourced outputs (89%), a system that learns from corrections (76%), structured refreshed competitive data (69%), and human oversight (61%).

The report concludes by positioning Klue's platform as providing the "intelligence and context layer" foundation that CI agents are missing. The page is authored by Renat Gersch and Hila Lauterbach, with Klue Labs headquartered in Vancouver, BC, © 2025.
</summary>

# AI-Powered Competitive Intelligence & Market Research Landscape (2025–2026)

The competitive intelligence (CI) and market research software market has entered a new phase of AI-native transformation. According to the [Crayon 2025 State of CI Report (8th Edition)](https://www.crayon.co/state-of-ci), **AI usage in CI jumped 76% year-over-year**, and 56% of CI professionals now use AI in their work, with 60% using it daily. The same report notes that **55% of companies say they have more competitive deals than they did a year ago**, and sellers face competitors in nearly 7 out of every 10 deals. This pressure has driven a 22% increase in CI budgets over $25K compared to 2024, and 54% of companies now have a dedicated CI platform (up 42% from last year) — these teams report 79% higher sales effectiveness.

This report profiles the five leading AI-powered CI and market research platforms in 2025–2026, with brief honorable mentions for additional notable products.

---

## 1. Crayon

### Core Capabilities and AI Features

Crayon is a competitive intelligence platform built for B2B sales and marketing teams to monitor competitors, enable sellers, and win more deals. Its platform is organized around four pillars: **Analyze** (competitor monitoring with AI news summarization, importance scoring, automated inbox insights), **Content Creation** (battlecards, announcements, newsletters), **Sales Enablement** (intelligence delivered in Salesforce, Slack, and other tools, with team leaderboards and 1:1 coaching), and **Performance Metrics** (win/loss analysis, engagement data, influenced revenue reporting).

Crayon monitors competitor websites for changes (pricing, messaging, product pages), aggregates news and social mentions, and applies AI summarization to translate raw monitoring into actionable insights. Its standout AI feature, **"Sparks"**, has become central to how customers consume CI. In 2026, Crayon also became the first CI vendor to launch an MCP (Model Context Protocol) Server, enabling deeper integration with AI assistants.

Customer outcomes on Crayon's homepage include a **40% increase in battlecard adoption** (Alteryx), **$6 million in influenced revenue in under a year** (Cognism), a **22% increase in competitive win rate** (Salsify), and a **win rate jump from 16% to 45%** (Affinity).

### Pricing Tiers

Crayon does **not** publish list pricing; all deals are quote-based and sales-led. Per [Vendr's analysis of 92 deals](https://www.vendr.com/marketplace/crayon) (last updated February 2026):

- **Median annual contract value:** $29,500 (range $12,600–$46,500+)
- **Average savings:** 20.19%

Three tiers are commonly negotiated:

- **Essentials** — entry-level, limited competitor tracking, basic analytics (~$15,000/year for 5–10 seats, 5–10 competitors)
- **Professional** — 10–25 competitors, advanced analytics, battlecard automation, CRM integrations
- **Enterprise** — 25+ competitors, custom integrations, API access, dedicated CSM, professional services ($100,000+/year)

Hidden costs that can add 15–30% to base pricing include onboarding/professional services (5–15% of ACV), battlecard customization, API access, additional competitor profiles, and annual price escalation clauses (typically 3–7%).

### Primary Target Customers

Enterprise and mid-market B2B SaaS companies with dedicated CI/PMM teams. Notable customers include Alteryx, Cognism, Salsify, Allego, Reputation, Affinity, Archer, Bloomerang, and Oyster.

### Key Differentiators

- Acquired by **SoftwareOne for $1.4B in July 2025**, creating a combined company operating in 70 countries with ~13,000 employees
- Named a **Leader in Gartner's inaugural Magic Quadrant for Competitive and Market Intelligence (CMI) Platforms (May 2026)**
- Won 3 consecutive PMA Pulse awards (2021–2023)
- Real-time alert strength across 100+ data types
- Native integrations with Salesforce, Slack, Gong, Highspot, ChatGPT, Glean, and Microsoft Copilot

### Official Website

[https://www.crayon.co](https://www.crayon.co)

---

## 2. Klue

### Core Capabilities and AI Features

Klue is a competitive enablement platform that combines win-loss analysis with competitive intelligence, bringing together objective buyer feedback. The platform operates in five stages: **Collect** (track competitive landscape with unlimited competitor coverage and a browser extension), **Analyze** (triage intel and add context), **Create** (battlecards, competitor profiles, executive dashboards), **Distribute** (deliver via Slack, Teams, Salesforce, email), and **Measure** (track content adoption, revenue impact, competitive landscape changes).

The standout AI capability is **Compete Agent**, an agentic AI system launched in 2025–2026 that delivers real-time competitive deal intelligence directly to sellers during active deals. Compete Agent includes a Research Analyst (continuous intel collection and competitor profile generation), a Competitive Deal Assistant (monitors competitive activity across open opportunities), Auto Insights, "Ask Klue" (instant answers in Slack, Salesforce, and Klue), Deal Tips (deal-specific insights to inboxes), and AI-generated Competitor Profiles. Klue also has **Auto Insights** that auto-generate competitive insights from CRM data, call recordings, win-loss interviews, and trusted external sources.

Klue's acquisitions of DoubleCheck (2022, win-loss), Goldpan.ai (2025, AI-driven win-loss research), and Ignition (2026, agentic product-marketing platform) have productized win-loss in a way no other CI vendor has matched.

Customer-reported outcomes on [Klue's homepage](https://klue.com) include a **35% increase in win rate**, **55% increase in average deal size**, and **75-day reduction in average sales cycle**. Dell Technologies reported expanding competitor coverage from 12 to 54 companies with the same team.

### Pricing Tiers

Klue does **not** publish pricing — all contracts are custom, sales-led, and require a demo. No free trial is available.

Per [Vendr's analysis of 82 deals](https://www.vendr.com/marketplace/klue) (last updated February 2026):

- **Average contract value:** $30,000
- **Average savings:** 17.63%

Deployment sizing:

- **Small teams** (5–15 users) typically pay mid-five figures (Essentials)
- **Mid-sized teams** (15–50 users) pay low-to-mid six figures (Professional)
- **Large deployments** (50+ users) reach mid-to-high six figures annually (Enterprise)

Per [SelectHub](https://www.selecthub.com/p/competitive-intelligence-software/klue/), pricing starts at $16,000 annually (quote-based). Hidden costs include onboarding (low-to-mid five figures), custom integrations, premium support, data source fees, mid-contract user expansion charges, and 5–10% annual renewal escalations.

### Primary Target Customers

B2B sales-led organizations with dedicated CI, product marketing, or sales enablement teams. Customers include Cisco, 3M, Drift, SAP, Dell Technologies, Blackbaud, Gainsight, Awardco, and Relias. The platform serves 250,000+ users and is best suited for medium-to-large businesses with dedicated CI or sales enablement functions.

### Key Differentiators

- Named a **Leader in Gartner's Magic Quadrant for CMI Platforms (May 2026)**
- Highest battlecard quality rating in the category (9.5/10 on G2)
- **94% user satisfaction** rating based on 461 aggregated reviews
- Recognized as a G2 Grid Leader, Momentum Leader, Easiest To Do Business With, Best Support, and Most Likely to Recommend (Spring 2025)
- Named to Deloitte's Fast 50 and Technology Fast 500 for four consecutive years (2021–2024)
- Strong Performer in Forrester's 2024 Market and Competitive Intelligence Wave Report
- Deep productized win-loss capabilities unmatched in the category

### Official Website

[https://klue.com](https://klue.com)

---

## 3. AlphaSense

### Core Capabilities and AI Features

AlphaSense is an AI-driven market research and intelligence platform that indexes more than **500 million premium documents**, including company filings, expert call transcripts, broker research, and market data across 37+ languages. Its **Smart Synonyms** technology automatically expands searches to include related terms, and its sentence-level citations reduce hallucinations while enabling one-click verification.

Recent AI platform enhancements include **Generative Search** (with Deep Research mode), **Generative Grid**, **Deep Research**, **Financial Data**, and an **AI Agent Interviewer**. The platform now offers AI workflow agents that function as domain-specific analysts, with coverage of 17,000+ companies, 115+ sector comp sets, 950,000+ M&A deals, and 685,000+ funding rounds. EU data residency is available for GDPR compliance.

AlphaSense is trusted by 7,000+ enterprises worldwide, including over 85% of the S&P 100 and 70% of the top 50 hedge funds. Notable customers include Cisco, Dow, Goldman Sachs, Microsoft, Salesforce, Siemens, Pfizer, and J.P. Morgan. The company was [named to Forbes' 2025 Cloud 100 list](https://www.alpha-sense.com/blog/alpha-sense-news/inc-best-in-business-2025/) and Inc.'s 2025 Best in Business list in "Best AI Implementation" and "Best in Innovation" categories. It was also positioned highest on Ability to Execute and furthest on Completeness of Vision in Gartner's inaugural CMI Magic Quadrant (May 2026).

### Pricing Tiers

AlphaSense does **not** publish public pricing; all plans require contacting sales for a custom quote. Annual subscriptions only.

Two main plans:

- **Market Intelligence** — company documents, broker and independent research (equity research from 1,000+ sell-side and independent firms), expert transcript library, news, regulatory content, 24/7 live help, dedicated account manager support, training, SaaS hosting
- **Enterprise Intelligence** — adds internal content integration, IT support, professional services, custom training programs, and private cloud hosting options

Product add-ons include **Expert Calls** and **Canalyst financial models**.

Per [SpendHound](https://spendhound.com/vendor/alpha-sense) (last updated June 25, 2026):

- **Average SMB pricing:** $44,754/year
- **Average enterprise pricing:** $125,124/year
- SMB pricing increased 17.81% YoY; enterprise pricing increased 48.36% YoY

Per [Sacra](https://sacra.com/c/alphasense/), pricing is typically $10,000–$20,000 per seat annually, with average deal sizes of $50,000–$100,000+ and enterprise deals exceeding $1M. ARR per customer grew from $28K to $66K in less than three years.

### Primary Target Customers

Financial analysts, corporate strategy teams, IR professionals, consultants, and market intelligence roles. Primary sectors include Financial Services (Investment Banking, Hedge Funds, Private Equity, Asset Management, Venture Capital), Corporations (Life Sciences & Healthcare, Tech/Media/Telecom, Energy, Industrials, Consumer Goods & Retail), and Consulting & Professional Services.

### Key Differentiators

- Recently surpassed **$500 million in ARR**, with Sacra estimating $600M ARR for March 2026 (73% YoY growth)
- **Valuation of $7.5 billion** (June 2026), up from $4 billion at Series E in June 2024
- Total funding exceeds $1.4 billion, with a $350M round led by Vitruvian Partners, Accenture Ventures, and J.P. Morgan Asset Management in June 2026
- Listed as No. 8 in the [CNBC Disruptor 50 in 2025](https://www.alpha-sense.com/blog/alpha-sense-news/cnbc-disruptor-50-2025/)
- Key acquisitions: **Tegus ($930M, July 2024)** for expanded expert transcripts, Sentieo (2022), Stream (2021), and Carousel (2025) for AI-native Excel modeling with 4,500 Canalyst models
- The only platform combining public and private financial data with expert call transcripts, broker research, and news in one workflow
- Tegus transcript library contains 100K–200,000+ transcripts covering 25,000 companies
- Enterprise Intelligence deal volume grew 185% YoY and is on track to grow 6x

### Official Website

[https://www.alpha-sense.com](https://www.alpha-sense.com)

---

## 4. Contify

### Core Capabilities and AI Features

Contify is an AI-native Market & Competitive Intelligence (M&CI) platform that delivers decision-ready insights about markets, industries, competitors, and key accounts. It provides access to over **1 million editorially curated sources**, including news, company websites, SEC filings, social media, regulatory portals, review sites, and job boards.

The platform's core AI engine is **Athena AI**, an agentic insights system with role-specific "Athena Agents" and "Ask Athena" for ad-hoc research. Contify uses a **Knowledge Graph** to extract and connect discrete data points and LLMs to derive actionable insights that address stakeholders' Key Intelligence Questions (KIQs).

Coverage spans **700,000+ companies**, **100+ industry segments**, and **200,000+ non-English sources** auto-translated from **117+ languages** — the broadest multilingual coverage in the CI category. The platform retains two years of historical data for trend analysis and applies a 7-step AI curation process to filter noise.

Five primary use cases include strategic decision-making, competitive product analysis, marketing strategy optimization, sales strategy support (battlecards, win-loss, account triggers), and role-specific insight sharing via AI. Internal source integration includes SharePoint, Slack, MS Teams, Email, Gong, and Fireflies. Native integrations include Slack, MS Teams, Salesforce, HubSpot, and Power BI.

Contify is AICPA SOC 2 certified, GDPR compliant, and supports SSO. Named a **Visionary in the 2026 Gartner Magic Quadrant for CMI Platforms**.

### Pricing Tiers

Contify does **not** publish list pricing; all deals are custom and quote-based. A **7-day free trial** is available.

Per [Improvado](https://improvado.io/blog/best-competitive-intelligence-tools), market intelligence hubs typically run $1,000–$2,500/month. Per [Debriefing](https://debriefing.io/blog/contify-pricing), mid-market pricing starts at ~$500/month, with mid-tier plans typically running $1,000–$3,000/month for teams of 5–10. Enterprise contracts typically range from $25,000–$70,000/year.

### Primary Target Customers

Mid-market companies with limited CI budgets; strategy, marketing, and business development teams; organizations needing complex intelligence routing across multiple business units; and teams prioritizing automated stakeholder briefings. Strongest sector presence in BFSI, pharma, IT, and consulting.

Notable clients include TSMC, Wipro, Merck, EY, Boston Consulting Group, Lenovo, BioCryst, Groww, Mahindra Comviva, Digital Realty, Galp, Esri, LexisNexis, and Northern Trust.

### Key Differentiators

- **Custom taxonomy system** — Contify's most distinctive feature — allowing teams to define custom tags, categories, and routing rules to direct intelligence to different stakeholders across business units
- **117+ languages** — broadest multilingual coverage in the category
- Athena AI engine launched in 2025, distinguishing it from retrofitted AI features at competitors
- 15+ years of historical data with deep archival coverage
- Managed services offering with bundled analyst hours for custom reporting
- Recognized 4 times by Gartner, plus Frost & Sullivan and Forrester
- Rated 4.5 on G2 based on 100+ reviews; G2 High Performer; SCIP member
- Typical deployment: 2 weeks setup time
- Customer outcomes: **$83M deal secured** by sales at a large IT company using Contify insights, **25 hours/week saved per analyst**, **30% reduction in manual research**, **50% improvement in competitor awareness**, **40% improvement in sales win rate**

### Official Website

[https://www.contify.com](https://www.contify.com)

## 5. Kompyte (by Semrush)

### Core Capabilities and AI Features

Kompyte is a competitive intelligence and sales enablement platform that automates competitor tracking across digital channels. It is primarily used by product marketing and sales enablement teams to centralize intelligence, create battlecards, and distribute them to sales.

Kompyte visits every competitor website and digital profile (social media, review sites, job postings, PR sites) once daily, using **supervised AI trained over 8 years** to classify changes and filter noise. The platform captures **500+ million data points** including competitor websites, Google News, PRNewswire, Glassdoor/Indeed/LinkedIn job postings, G2/Capterra/TrustRadius reviews, app stores, SERP/SEO monitoring, AdWords, and Crunchbase.

Features include unlimited battlecards integrated with HubSpot and Salesforce, two-way Salesforce integration for win/loss analysis, InMail win-loss interviews, and **GPT Auto Summarize** for instant takeaways. Subsequent updates added AI-powered battlecard auto-writing, IcebergIQ win/loss interview integration with GPT summaries, and support for tracking up to **200 companies** with SSO and one-click dashboards.

Kompyte was acquired by Semrush and is now positioned as the CI and sales enablement arm of Semrush.

### Pricing Tiers

Kompyte does **not** publish pricing; all contracts are custom and sales-led. Per [Autobound](https://www.autobound.ai/blog/best-competitive-intelligence-tools), most affordable pricing starts from $300/year (likely a base/limited plan for very small teams). Per [Parano.ai](https://parano.ai/competitive-intelligence-tools/), Kompyte is the cheapest of the enterprise CI suites at comparable deployment sizes, with entry deployments starting at $15,000–$20,000+/year for typical use cases. Pricing is per-competitor and per-user, which can become costly as teams scale.

### Primary Target Customers

**Lean PMM teams** (1–2 people) at mid-market companies. Kompyte is positioned as a "Best Value" pick with a price-friendly entry point, strong capture capabilities, and a 24-hour setup time. Customers tend to outgrow it as their CI programs mature.

### Key Differentiators

- Founded in 2014 in Barcelona; acquired by Semrush for ~$10M
- **Cheapest of the major enterprise CI suites** at entry tier
- Mature AI, fast setup (24 hours), and recent Semrush backing
- Customers report **30% average increase in win rates**
- Strong fit for sales-enablement-only use cases with a dedicated curator

### Official Website

[https://www.kompyte.com](https://www.kompyte.com)

## Honorable Mentions

### Similarweb

Similarweb is a publicly traded digital intelligence platform (NYSE: SMWB) with strong AI-powered CI and market research features. Its AI capabilities include **GenAI Intelligence** (citation analysis showing actual sources used in AI chatbot answers, sentiment analysis, tracking AI industry leaders like ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude), AI Traffic Trends, AI Brand Visibility, Top Landing Pages, and Prompt Analysis, with up to 37 months of historical data.

**Pricing** is split across separately-priced modules:

- **Web Intelligence — Competitive Intelligence tier:** $125/month annually ($199/month month-to-month)
- **+ SEO + AEO:** $333/month annually ($399/month month-to-month)
- **+ Ads:** $542/month annually ($649/month month-to-month)
- **Retail Intelligence:** Starter $15K/year, Business $25K/year
- **Sales Intelligence (Individual):** $99/year

A 7-day free trial is available, plus a limited free tier. Per Vendr's 157-purchase dataset, the median buyer pays $37,800/year (range $14,400–$87,900). Trusted by over 10,000 customers including adidas, DHL, eBay, Ogilvy, Walmart, and Samsonite.

**Website:** [https://www.similarweb.com](https://www.similarweb.com)

### WatchMyCompetitor (WMC)

WMC is an enterprise AI platform for external intelligence, tracking **1 billion+ data points daily** with 14+ specialist AI agents and 24/7 continuous monitoring. Its agents cover Product, Marketing, Sales, Strategy, Pricing, Leadership, and Client Success, with a distinctive "Human Validated" approach where analysts verify, summarize, categorize, and prioritize every update.

WMC tracks over 45 million data points and saves clients approximately 70,000 man-hours per month. Over 3,500 leaders and decision-makers use the platform globally. Recognized in Gartner's 2026 Magic Quadrant for CMI Tools. Notable clients include Santander, E.ON, Aviva, Novo Nordisk, Honeywell, Abbott, ThermoFisher, ABB, Reckitt, Hugo Boss, Unilever, Umbro, and Royal Marsden Hospital.

Pricing is quote-based, demo-only, and not publicly disclosed.

**Website:** [https://www.watchmycompetitor.com](https://www.watchmycompetitor.com)

### G2

G2 is a software marketplace with user reviews and ratings that has become an essential meta-tool for CI vendors. Klue's "Klue Insights from G2 reviews and win-loss interviews" automatically generates competitor strengths and weaknesses from online reviews across multiple platforms. G2 itself is free to use.

**Website:** [https://www.g2.com](https://www.g2.com)

## Key Market Takeaways

1. **AI adoption in CI is now mainstream.** Per Crayon's 2025 State of CI Report, 56% of CI professionals use AI in their work, with 60% using it daily — and teams deploying AI see an 82% boost in sales effectiveness.

2. **The market is consolidating around a few leaders.** Crayon (acquired by SoftwareOne for $1.4B), Klue, and AlphaSense are the three most prominent platforms, all named as Leaders in Gartner's inaugural CMI Magic Quadrant (May 2026).

3. **Budgets are rebounding.** CI budgets increased 22% YoY for budgets over $25K, with 54% of companies now operating dedicated CI platforms.

4. **Agentic AI is the next frontier.** Klue's Compete Agent, Contify's Athena AI, and AlphaSense's AI Agent Interviewer represent the shift from AI-assisted search to autonomous, workflow-embedded intelligence delivery.

5. **Most enterprise CI platforms remain quote-based.** Of the five products profiled, none publish list pricing, reflecting the customization and integration complexity typical of enterprise CI deployments.

6. **Mid-market options exist at lower price points.** Contify and Kompyte offer more accessible entry pricing for leaner teams, though feature depth and AI sophistication vary accordingly.
